title: Film Filing and Storage of Processed Radiographs
narrationScript: Here is a fact that surprises a lot of new Level two technicians. The radiograph you shot this morning may need to be readable twenty or thirty years from now. A radiograph is not just a picture. It is a quality record, and on many contracts it is a legal document. It may be pulled for a code audit, a failure investigation, a warranty claim, or a fitness for service review long after the weld has cooled and the paint has dried. Codes and contracts set the retention period, and it ranges from a year or two on routine commercial work to the life of the component on nuclear and critical service jobs. So this lesson is about three things. How we process a film so it is capable of surviving that long. How we store it so it actually does survive. And how we file it so a stranger can find it in minutes instead of days. Start with what kills a radiograph from the inside. The metallic silver image on a well processed film is remarkably stable. What fades and stains radiographs is leftover processing chemistry, above all residual thiosulfate, the fixer chemical most of us simply call hypo. If fixing ran long in tired fixer, or the wash was cut short, thiosulfate stays behind in the emulsion. Over months and years it reacts with the silver image and converts it to yellowish brown silver sulfide. The background stains, the image fades, and the finest details, like the thinnest image quality indicator wire, are the first to go. Heat and humidity speed the reaction up, but the seed was planted on processing day. Retention life is determined in the darkroom, not in the archive. Because residual thiosulfate is invisible, we measure it. Two standardized tests are common. The methylene blue method is the laboratory referee. It measures the actual thiosulfate concentration in the emulsion, reported in micrograms per square centimeter. The silver densitometric method, often packaged as a spot test or hypo estimator, is the practical shop check. A drop of test solution goes on a clear area of the processed film, sits for a set time, and is blotted off. The more hypo remains, the darker the stain it leaves, and the stain is compared with calibrated reference patches. Guides for archival film keeping set tight limits, on the order of two micrograms per square centimeter. If your films test high, the cure is upstream. Keep the fixer fresh and properly replenished. Do not overload it. Use a hypo clearing bath where your procedure allows one. And wash in running water, with several complete changes per hour, for the full time at the right temperature. Now the archive itself. Processed film wants a cool, dry, stable, clean home. Typical practice holds the room somewhere around sixty to seventy five degrees Fahrenheit, with relative humidity between about thirty and fifty percent. High humidity grows mold and glues films together. Very dry air makes film brittle and invites static. Swings are as bad as extremes, because temperature cycling pumps moisture in and out of the emulsion. Keep film away from chemical fumes. Sulfide gases, ammonia, paint vapors, and cleaning solvents all attack the silver image. Keep it away from radiation too. Unexposed film stock stored near an exposure bay or a source locker fogs slowly and silently. Once a film is fixed, its silver halide is gone and it can no longer fog, but keeping the archive clear of radiation areas is still good practice. Many operations use fire resistant cabinets or a records vault, because a single fire can erase a decade of quality history. How the film sits matters as much as the room. File radiographs on edge, standing upright in envelopes, never in flat stacks where the weight presses films together and any defect prints through the pile. Use storage envelopes made for archives, low sulfur paper with the adhesive at the edges only, or seamless sleeves, because a glue seam running across the face of an envelope will stain the film pressed against it years later. Separate films that share an envelope with acid free interleaving paper. No rubber bands, no paper clips, no tape. Finally, filing and separation. An archive you cannot search is just a scrap pile with a thermostat. Every film carries identification tying it to the job, the part or weld number, the date, and the view, and that identification must match the reader sheet and the report. A master index records exactly which cabinet, drawer, and envelope holds each film. Separate the holdings by contract and by retention date, so records can be handed to the customer or destroyed on schedule without resorting the entire room. File repair films beside their originals so the history reads as one story. Then audit the archive on a schedule. Pull sample films, look for yellowing, sticking, and mold, and prove you can produce any radiograph an auditor names in minutes. That is what professional record keeping looks like.
imagePrompt: A technician in a climate-controlled records room slides a labeled film envelope into a row of vertically filed radiograph envelopes inside an open fire-rated steel cabinet, a wall hygrometer and thermometer visible beside the shelving.

sections.heading: A Radiograph Is a Quality Record
sections.body:
A finished radiograph is not just the means to an accept-or-reject call - it is a **quality record**, and on many contracts a legal one. Years after a weld is buried, painted, or flying, that film may be pulled for a code audit, a failure investigation, a warranty dispute, or a fitness-for-service review. Codes such as ASME and the governing contract set the **retention period**: a year or two is common on routine commercial work, while nuclear, aerospace, and critical-service jobs can require retention for the life of the component. The radiography organization is expected to produce any requested film - with its reader sheet and technique record, identification intact - in minutes, not days.

That expectation drives everything in this lesson. The film must be **processed** for permanence, **stored** under conditions that preserve it, and **filed** so it can be found. Miss any one of the three and the archive fails its purpose: a faded film, a moldy film, and a lost film all look the same to an auditor - like a missing record.

sections.heading: Retention Life Is Set in the Darkroom
sections.body:
The metallic-silver image on a properly processed film is remarkably stable. What destroys radiographs from the inside is leftover processing chemistry - chiefly **residual thiosulfate**, the fixer chemical universally called hypo. When fixing runs long in exhausted fixer, or washing is cut short, thiosulfate remains in the emulsion. Over months to years it reacts with the silver image to form yellow-brown **silver sulfide**: the background stains, the image fades, and the finest detail - the thinnest IQI wire, the hairline crack image - disappears first. Heat and humidity accelerate the reaction, but the seed was planted on processing day.

Retention life is therefore controlled upstream:
- **Fix correctly** - fresh, properly replenished fixer; do not exceed the film load the bath is rated for; fix for the recommended time (commonly about twice the clearing time), not longer.
- **Use a hypo-clearing agent** where the procedure allows - it shortens the wash and improves thiosulfate removal.
- **Wash thoroughly** - running water with several complete changes per hour, typically 20 to 30 minutes at processing temperature, with hangers spaced so water reaches every surface.

A film that leaves the darkroom chemically clean can be archived; one that leaves dirty cannot be rescued later.

sections.heading: Measuring Retention Life
sections.body:
Residual thiosulfate is invisible, so archival quality is **measured**, not assumed. Two standardized tests dominate:

- **Methylene blue method** - the laboratory referee test. It chemically measures the thiosulfate ion concentration in the emulsion, reported in micrograms per square centimeter.
- **Silver densitometric (spot) test** - the practical shop check, often packaged as a hypo estimator kit. A drop of test solution is placed on a clear area of the processed film, left for a fixed time, then blotted. Residual hypo converts the drop to brown silver sulfide - the **darker the stain, the more hypo remains** - and the stain is compared against calibrated reference patches.

Guides for film permanence and radiograph storage set residual-thiosulfate limits for archival keeping on the order of **2 micrograms per square centimeter**. A sound written practice tests a sample film periodically - and any time fixer or wash conditions change. If results trend high, the cure is in the processing room: refresh or re-replenish the fixer, verify fixing time, extend or repair the wash, then re-test before trusting the next batch to the archive.

sections.caption: Retention-life testing: residual thiosulfate left by poor fixing or washing is detected with a spot test and compared against calibrated stain patches.

sections.heading: Long-Term Storage Conditions
sections.body:
Once the film is archivally clean, the storage room decides whether it stays that way. The targets are **cool, dry, stable, and clean**:

- **Temperature** - a controlled room around 60 to 75 °F; cooler is better for very long retention. Avoid attics, sea containers, and anywhere that cycles hot and cold: temperature swings pump moisture in and out of the emulsion.
- **Relative humidity** - hold roughly **30 to 50 %**. Above about 60 % RH, mold grows and emulsions stick together; very dry air makes film brittle and invites static.
- **Chemical atmosphere** - keep films away from sulfide gases, ammonia, paint and solvent vapors, and the processing room itself. Fumes attack the silver image just as residual hypo does.
- **Radiation** - never store unexposed film stock near an exposure bay or source storage; it fogs silently. Once fixed, processed film can no longer fog - but keep the archive clear of radiation areas as well.
- **Fire** - quality records belong in fire-resistant cabinets or a vault; one fire can erase a decade of compliance.

A wall thermometer and hygrometer, read and logged on a schedule, turn these targets from good intentions into objective evidence.

sections.caption: An archival film room: radiographs filed on edge in labeled envelopes inside fire-resistant cabinets, with temperature and humidity controlled and logged.

sections.title: The working test of an archive is retrieval
sections.body: An auditor names a weld; you produce the radiograph, reader sheet, and technique record **in minutes**. Index every film, audit the room on a schedule - pull sample films and look for yellowing, sticking, or mold - and when something is found, fix the system, not just the film.

sections.heading: Filing and Separation Techniques
sections.body:
Filing covers both how films physically sit and how they are organized.

**Physical separation.** File radiographs **on edge** - standing vertically in envelopes - never in flat stacks, where weight presses films together, prints any defect through the pile, and makes the bottom film unreachable. Use archival-quality envelopes: low-sulfur paper with **adhesive seams at the edges only**, or seamless sleeves, because a glue line crossing the face of an envelope will stain the film against it years later. Films sharing an envelope get acid-free interleaving paper between them. No rubber bands, no paper clips, no pressure-sensitive tape, and never write on an envelope with the film inside it.

**Organizational separation.** Every film carries identification - job or contract number, part or weld number, date, and view - matching the reader sheet and report. A master index (logbook or database) records which cabinet, drawer, and envelope holds each film. Separate holdings **by contract and by retention date**, so records can be transferred to the customer or destroyed on schedule without re-sorting the room, and file repair films (R1, R2) with their original shots so the history reads as one story.

sections.caption: Archive enemies and the defense against each.
rows: Forms silver sulfide - stain and image fade
Proper fixing and washing; spot test / methylene blue checks
Store away from processing and chemical areas
Store film stock far from bays and source storage
Film exists but cannot be produced
Labels plus master index; separation by contract and retention date

checkQuestions.question: Years after processing, an archived radiograph develops an overall yellow-brown stain and its fine detail fades. The most likely root cause is:
options: Excess residual thiosulfate left by inadequate fixing or washing
Storing the film on edge instead of flat
Too much developer replenisher used on processing day
Viewing the film too often on a high-intensity illuminator
checkQuestions.explanation: Residual thiosulfate (hypo) slowly converts the metallic-silver image to yellow-brown silver sulfide, staining the background and fading the image - and the failure was built in by poor fixing or washing on processing day.

checkQuestions.question: In the silver densitometric spot test, the test drop leaves a noticeably dark stain on the film. This indicates:
options: Low residual thiosulfate - excellent archival quality
High residual thiosulfate - shortened retention life
The film was overdeveloped during processing
The film has unusually high speed
checkQuestions.explanation: Residual hypo converts the test drop's silver to brown silver sulfide, so a darker stain means more thiosulfate remains; the stain is compared with calibrated patches against an archival limit on the order of 2 micrograms per square centimeter.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of conditions is best for long-term storage of processed radiographs?
options: 85 °F and 70 % RH to keep the emulsion supple
About 60-75 °F and 30-50 % RH, stable, away from chemical fumes and radiation
Below freezing with weekly thaw cycles for inspection
Any conditions, provided the films are stacked flat and tightly
checkQuestions.explanation: A cool, dry, stable, clean room - roughly 60-75 °F at 30-50 % RH, free of fumes and stray radiation - preserves film; heat, high humidity, cycling, and chemical vapors all attack the image.

checkQuestions.question: Why must archival film envelopes have adhesive at the edges only (or no seams at all)?
options: Edge-glued envelopes are cheaper to manufacture
A glue seam crossing the face of the envelope can stain the film pressed against it during storage
Center seams make envelopes too stiff to file on edge
Adhesive position only matters for unexposed film
checkQuestions.explanation: Adhesives in contact with the emulsion react with the image over time, printing a stain line along the seam - so archival envelopes keep glue at the edges or eliminate seams entirely.

checkQuestions.question: An auditor asks for the radiograph of weld W-31, shot three years ago. A filing system that meets its purpose lets you:
options: Locate the film, reader sheet, and technique record within minutes using the master index
Begin a search of the flat stacks and promise an answer within a week
Re-shoot the weld to recreate the missing record
Produce a film of a similar weld as a substitute
checkQuestions.explanation: Identification on every film tied to a master index - separated by contract and retention date - is what makes the archive a usable quality record; anything less is storage, not filing.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course §2.6 (film filing and storage)
ASTM-style guidance on storage and retention of processed radiographs (residual thiosulfate limits, storage environment - generic reference)
ISO/ANSI photographic film permanence standards (methylene blue and silver densitometric test methods - generic reference)
